metadata.source: Species detail enrichment overlay for reserved batch plants-b

records.scientific_name: Vaccinium spp. (lowbush/highbush types in Upper Michigan)
records.culinary_uses: Ripe berries are excellent fresh, dried, frozen, or cooked into pancakes, muffins, pies, jam, syrup, fruit leather, and wine. Leaves are not a food, but berries can also be dehydrated for winter use.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional food-medicine berry rich in anthocyanins and tannins; commonly used as a nourishing fruit rather than a strong herb. Leaves have also been used in folk tea traditions, but the berries are the main practical medicinal part here.
records.notes: Low shrubs to taller bushes depending on species; common in acidic barrens, conifer edges, sandy openings, and bog margins. Fruit is best harvested by hand or combed lightly without stripping all berries from a patch. Taste and berry size vary with species and site. Sustainable harvest means leaving plenty for wildlife and avoiding trampling fragile barrens or wetland edges.

records.culinary_uses: The soft red berries are pleasant fresh but fragile, so they are often better eaten on the trail or quickly turned into jam, jelly, syrup, fruit leather, or freezer preserves. The large tender spring leaves can also be used as wrappers in some traditional food uses.
records.medicinal_uses: Leaves and roots have astringent traditional use, especially as teas for mouth, throat, or digestive complaints. In practical modern use, the fruit is the easiest and safest part to rely on.
records.notes: Thornless raspberry relative with huge soft maple-like leaves and white flowers. Usually found in cool moist edges, burns, roadsides, and openings, often in partial shade. Fruit crushes easily, so collect in shallow containers. Good patch species for gentle harvesting because the berries do not store long.

records.culinary_uses: Ripe purple fruits are excellent fresh and are also one of the best wild berries for pies, cobblers, jam, syrup, fruit leather, and drying. They were historically mixed with fat and meat in pemmican and also work well in muffins and pancakes.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food plant, though the fruits are valued as an antioxidant-rich nourishing berry. Bark and roots have ethnobotanical history, but the fruit is the part most worth emphasizing in this guide.
records.notes: Shrub or small tree with white spring flowers before or with leaves, then sweet red-to-purple fruit in early summer. Common on woodland edges, rocky openings, old fields, and roadsides. Fruit ripens unevenly and disappears fast to birds. Sustainable harvest means taking from multiple shrubs instead of stripping one clean.

records.culinary_uses: Tiny berries are one of the best fresh trail snacks and can also be used for jam, syrup, shortcake, fruit leather, infused vinegar, and desserts. Young leaves are sometimes dried for tea, though the fruit is the main foraging prize.
records.medicinal_uses: A gentle folk tea plant, especially from the leaves, but in practice it is most useful as a nourishing edible berry. The fruit is aromatic, lightly acidic, and vitamin-rich.
records.notes: Low runner-forming groundcover with white five-petaled flowers and small very fragrant red fruit. Usually in sunny openings, old fields, roadsides, sandy soil, and light woodland edges. The main real look-alike issue is mock strawberry: yellow flowers mean not this plant. Harvest lightly because small patches can be picked clean very fast.

records.culinary_uses: Tart berries are excellent for pies, jam, jelly, chutney, syrup, and sauce; sweeter fully ripe fruits can also be eaten fresh. They cook down well and often need sugar more than other berries.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food fruit in modern use. Some traditions also used roots or stems medicinally, but the fruit is the main practical part for this app.
records.notes: Spiny currant-family shrubs of thickets, stream margins, cool openings, and northern woods. Fruit color can vary by species from green to red, purple, or blackish. The spines make harvest slower than currants. Some Ribes are alternate hosts for white pine blister rust, so know local regulations and avoid moving live plants around.

records.culinary_uses: Only ripe cooked berries should be used, usually for syrup, jelly, wine, cordial, pancakes, or pies. Flowers can also be used for fritters or cordial in some traditions, but the fruit is the main preserved part.
records.medicinal_uses: Ripe cooked berry preparations are widely used in folk medicine for seasonal colds and as a dark antioxidant-rich tonic. Flowers also have a long history in tea for feverish colds. Keep claims modest and practical.
records.notes: Opposite leaves, flat or rounded clusters of many berries, and pithy stems on shrub-like plants of wet edges, openings, and disturbed ground. Important caution: raw or unripe berries and the leaves, bark, stems, and seeds can cause stomach upset or worse; cook ripe fruit and avoid casual nibbling. Leave plenty for birds because elder patches are major wildlife food.

records.culinary_uses: The bright red fruits are usually too tart, musky, or astringent for casual fresh eating but make good jelly, sauce, syrup, chutney, and fruit leather, especially after frost or freezing. Straining out the seeds improves texture and flavor.
records.medicinal_uses: Bark of related viburnums has traditional cramp-relief use, though the fruit is the more practical edible part here. The berries are also valued as a vitamin-rich preserving fruit.
records.notes: Large shrub with opposite three-lobed leaves, flat lacecap flower clusters, and hanging red fruit in late season. Best patches are on moist edges, thickets, low woods, and old farm places. The American plant is the preferred edible one; some European lookalikes are more bitter and smell stronger. Fruit often hangs into winter and sweetens after freezes.

records.culinary_uses: Strong-flavored berries are excellent for jelly, syrup, juice, cordial, wine, fruit leather, and rich jam; some people enjoy them fresh when fully ripe. Leaves can also be dried for tea in some traditions.
records.medicinal_uses: Currants are mainly nourishing fruits, but black-fruited currants are especially valued in folk use for vitamin C and dark-pigment antioxidants. Leaf teas also appear in traditional herbal use.
records.notes: Currant-family shrubs without the heavy stem spines of many gooseberries. Fruit hangs in small clusters. Black currants usually have a stronger resinous aroma than red currants. Harvest by cluster when fully ripe and leave under-ripe fruit to color up. As with other Ribes, be mindful of white pine blister rust concerns around live plant movement.

records.culinary_uses: Translucent tart berries are classic jelly and syrup fruit and also work in sauces, pies, currant leather, fermented drinks, and mixed berry preserves. They can be eaten fresh when ripe but are usually more useful cooked.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a nourishing food berry with folk use as a cooling, vitamin-rich fruit. Leaves and flowers have also been used in tea traditions, but the fruit is the main practical part.
records.notes: Small shrubs with lobed leaves and dangling strings of red berries, usually in cool moist edges or old garden escapes. Red currants are generally less resinous than black currants. The fruit is delicate and easiest to strip from clusters into a bowl. Birds love them, so timing matters.

records.culinary_uses: Very astringent raw berries are usually cooked into juice, jelly, syrup, wine, fruit leather, chutney, or mixed preserves with sweeter fruit. Their high pectin and dark juice make them useful in blends.
records.medicinal_uses: A modern food-medicine berry prized for deep pigment, polyphenols, and antioxidant reputation. In real-world use it functions best as a tart preserving fruit rather than a stand-alone medicine.
records.notes: Cold-hardy suckering shrub with white spring flowers, black fruits, and intense red fall color. Often used in wet sites, wildlife plantings, and hedges. Fruit is best after full ripening and often after frost or freezing. The mouth-drying astringency is normal. Do not judge it by fresh-snacking standards.

records.culinary_uses: Fully ripe fruits are usually cooked and strained for jelly, syrup, wine, and sauce rather than eaten by the handful. The flesh is useful, but the pits are not chewed or crushed because cherry-family seeds contain cyanogenic compounds.
records.medicinal_uses: Bark and fruit both have strong ethnobotanical history, but the fruit is the safest practical foraging part in this guide when properly prepared. Use food amounts, not heroic herbal dosing.
records.notes: Large shrub or small tree with long drooping flower racemes in spring and dark red-to-black fruits later. Common on edges, roadsides, old fields, and disturbed ground. The raw fruit is puckery until dead ripe. Critical caution: avoid crushing or blending the pits into food, and do not use wilted leaves internally.

records.culinary_uses: Mealy fruits are most useful as jelly, ketchup, fruit paste, syrup, leather, vinegar, or tea decoctions rather than as prime fresh fruit. Some species are sweeter than others, but all are seed-heavy.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic heart-tonic plant in Western herbalism, especially from berries, flowers, and leaves. For this guide, keep it in the realm of gentle traditional support and avoid strong claims for anyone on heart or blood-pressure medication.
records.notes: Spiny shrub or small tree with white spring flowers, lobed leaves, and red fruits in fall. Good hedgerow and edge species. Harvest carefully because the thorns are no joke. Fruit texture is usually dry or mealy, so preserving is more rewarding than fresh snacking.

records.culinary_uses: Leaves and berries can be used for tea, candying, flavoring, infused syrup, or chewing for their strong wintergreen taste. The berries are edible but mild compared with the leaves; many people prize the aroma more than the bulk food value.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional anti-inflammatory and pain-related herb because the plant contains salicylate compounds. This is one to treat with respect, not as an everyday free-for-all snack herb. Avoid large internal use if aspirin-sensitive or on blood thinners.
records.notes: Low evergreen woodland creeper of acidic conifer or mixed woods with glossy leaves and bright red berries that often persist into winter. Crushing the leaves releases the unmistakable wintergreen smell. Good low-volume foraging species: take a little from many patches, because it spreads slowly and patches are easy to overpick. Berries can be confused with partridgeberry by beginners, but the leaf shape, scent, and plant habit are different.

records.culinary_uses: The soft new spring tips make an excellent citrusy tea and can also be used in syrup, infused sugar, jelly, soda, or beer-like ferments. Small amounts can brighten fish, game, or desserts much like a resinous lemon zest.
records.medicinal_uses: Best treated as a vitamin-rich aromatic tea ingredient with traditional respiratory-season use rather than a heavy medicinal herb. The pleasant resin-citrus brew is the main practical use.
records.notes: Evergreen conifer with short flat needles on tiny stalks and drooping branchlets; new tips are soft and bright green in spring. This is a tree, not the poisonous herb poison-hemlock. Harvest only a little from each branch tip so you do not deform young trees, and avoid stripping one sapling heavily. Best flavor is when tips are still tender.

records.culinary_uses: Tender spring tips are one of the best northern flavorings for tea, syrup, jelly, spruce beer, infused honey, vinegar, sugar, salt, or short-term pickles. They can also be chopped into butter, desserts, and fish or game glazes.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly used as an aromatic vitamin-rich tea or seasonal tonic rather than as a strong medicine. Traditional use leans toward cough, sore-throat, and general winter/northern camp remedies.
records.notes: Soft pale green new growth appears at branch ends in spring before the needles toughen. Flavor is resinous, citrusy, and bright when tips are young, then quickly becomes tougher and less pleasant. Harvest lightly across many branches and many trees rather than stripping one. Choose clean trees away from roads and spray zones.
